subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
#### Main Components
- **1** fairground game board (circular track with 6 ticket spaces)
- **84** vision cards (oversized, beautifully illustrated dream cards)
- **13** character cards (suspects)
- **12** location cards (places in the park)
- **1** ghost screen (hides the ghost player's information)

#### Tokens & Markers
- **Ticket tokens** — track rounds remaining (6 rounds max)
- **Innocence tokens** — mark eliminated suspects/locations
- **Psychic tokens** — 1 per psychic player
- **Ghost tokens** — for the ghost player

#### Setup Cards
- **Plot cards** — secret combinations the ghost knows (1 character + 1 location per psychic)

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
1. **Place the fairground board** — Center of the table.
2. **Character ring** — Arrange character cards in a circle around the board (or in a row). These are the suspects.
3. **Location ring** — Arrange location cards similarly. These are the possible crime scenes.
4. **Ghost screen** — The ghost player sets up their screen to hide secret information.
5. **Ticket track** — Place the ticket marker on space 1 (of 6).
6. **Vision cards** — The ghost takes all vision cards behind their screen and draws a hand of 7.
7. **Plot setup** — Behind the screen, the ghost secretly assigns one character and one location to each psychic player.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Ghost Player
- **1 player** is the ghost. They know the truth but **cannot speak** during the game.
- Draw **plot cards** that assign a specific character and location to each psychic.
- Take the vision card deck and draw a hand of **7 vision cards**.

#### Psychic Players
- All other players are **psychics** trying to solve the mystery.
- Each psychic has a **character assignment** (unknown to them) and a **location assignment** (unknown to them).
- Psychics can freely discuss and help each other interpret the ghost's clues.

#### Communication Rule
- The ghost **cannot talk, gesture, or indicate** anything except by giving vision cards.
- Psychics may talk freely among themselves.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
- **Phase 1 starts** — Character identification phase.
- **6 rounds maximum** — Tracked by tickets on the fairground.
- **Goal** — In Phase 1, each psychic must correctly identify their assigned character. In Phase 2, identify the location.
- **Cooperative** — All players win or lose together.
- The ghost gives vision cards as clues at the start of each round.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Phase 1: Character Identification (Rounds 1-6)
Each round:
1. **Ghost gives clues** — The ghost selects 1 or more vision cards from their hand and places them face-up in front of each psychic. These cards are abstract clues hinting at which character is assigned to that psychic.
2. **Psychics discuss** — All psychics look at their vision cards and discuss together which character each card might be pointing toward.
3. **Psychics guess** — Each psychic places their token on a character card.
4. **Ghost reveals** — The ghost indicates correct/incorrect for each psychic.
5. **Correct guesses** — That character is eliminated from the suspect pool and the psychic advances to Phase 2 (or waits).
6. **Incorrect guesses** — That psychic tries again next round with new vision cards. A ticket is consumed.
7. **Ghost draws** — The ghost draws back up to 7 vision cards.

#### Phase 2: Location Identification
Once all psychics have identified their characters (or time runs out), Phase 2 begins. Same process but for locations instead of characters.

#### Final Revelation
Eliminated characters and locations narrow down the true culprit. Remaining non-eliminated pairs form the final choices. Psychics must agree on the murderer from the remaining options.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Ghost Actions
- **Select vision cards** — Choose cards from your hand that abstractly represent the target character or location. The art on vision cards is surreal and open to interpretation.
- **Discard and redraw** — You may discard your entire hand and draw 7 new cards (costs a ticket/round).

#### Psychic Actions
- **Interpret clues** — Study the vision cards for colors, objects, themes, or moods that connect to a suspect or location.
- **Discuss** — Talk with other psychics about what you see. Collaboration is encouraged.
- **Vote/Guess** — Place your marker on your chosen character or location.

#### Elimination
- Each correct guess **eliminates** that character/location from the suspect pool.
- This narrows down the final options, making the end game easier.
- Each incorrect guess wastes a round ticket.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### Vision Cards
- Large, beautifully illustrated cards with **abstract, dreamlike imagery**.
- No text — purely visual.
- The ghost must creatively select cards whose imagery evokes the target character or location.
- Examples: A card with fire might hint at a fire-breather character or a bonfire location.

#### Ticket Track (6 Rounds)
- You have a maximum of **6 rounds** to complete both phases.
- Each round where at least one psychic guesses wrong costs a ticket.
- If all tickets are used before both phases complete, the team has fewer clues for the final revelation.

#### Final Revelation
- After both phases, the remaining (non-eliminated) characters and locations form the possible culprit pairs.
- The ghost gives one final set of vision cards.
- All psychics must **agree** on which remaining pair is the true murderer and location.
- If correct: **everyone wins**.
- If incorrect: **everyone loses**.

#### Streamlined from Mysterium
- No weapon phase (original Mysterium had characters, locations, AND weapons).
- Fewer rounds and faster gameplay.
- Same core deduction mechanic with abstract vision cards.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### Win Condition
The team **wins** if the psychics correctly identify the true murderer and location in the final revelation.

#### Lose Condition
The team **loses** if:
- Psychics choose the wrong culprit pair in the final revelation.
- Optional: The team can also lose if they use all 6 tickets without completing both phases (depending on variant).

#### Difficulty Scaling
- **Fewer psychics** = harder (less collaboration, fewer eliminations).
- **More suspects/locations** = harder (more options to narrow down).
- The game has no numeric score — it is purely win or lose.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
- **Ghost: Study your hand** — Before giving clues, look at all 7 cards and find the best matches for each psychic's target.
- **Ghost: Use color and theme** — Obvious connections (color matching, similar objects) work better than abstract thematic links.
- **Psychics: Look at ALL clues** — Compare what other psychics received to narrow options.
- **Start with distinctive characters** — If one character is very different from others, target them first for easy elimination.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
- **Ghost: Being too subtle** — Abstract connections that make sense to you may not make sense to others. Be as clear as possible.
- **Ghost: Giving too many cards** — 1-2 well-chosen cards are better than 3-4 vague ones.
- **Psychics: Overthinking** — The most obvious interpretation is usually correct.
- **Psychics: Not collaborating** — Discuss openly. Fresh eyes often see connections you miss.
- **Both: Ignoring elimination** — Pay attention to what's already eliminated. It narrows every future guess.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
- **Ghost: Which cards to give** — Your only meaningful decision. Choose clarity over cleverness.
- **Ghost: When to discard hand** — Discarding costs a round but gets fresh cards. Worth it if your hand has nothing useful.
- **Psychics: Confidence level** — If unsure, a wrong guess wastes a round. Sometimes waiting for better clues is smarter.
- **Final revelation** — The most critical decision. Discuss thoroughly and consider all remaining options before committing.
